document: We review medical image segmentation methods that inform our boundary-aware approach. Early pipelines relied on atlas registration and deformable models to delineate anatomy. Convolutional networks replaced hand-crafted features with hierarchical representations. Skip connections improve localization by fusing low-level and high-level features. Loss functions beyond cross-entropy address class imbalance and shape priors. Data augmentation is crucial given scarce labeled volumes and inter-scanner variability. Multimodal fusion integrates complementary information from MR sequences or CT phases. Test-time adaptation mitigates domain shift across sites and protocols. Recent work studies uncertainty estimation for safety-critical predictions. Semi-supervised and self-supervised learning reduce annotation requirements substantially. Benchmarking practices emphasize standardized preprocessing and robust validation splits. Ronneberger et al. (2015) introduced U-Net for biomedical segmentation. Çiçek et al. (2016) extended the architecture to volumetric data. Milletari et al. (2016) proposed V-Net with Dice loss. Zhou et al. (2018) presented UNet++ with nested skip pathways. Isensee et al. (2018) released nnU-Net as a self-configuring framework baseline. These architectures motivate design choices for receptive field, depth, and normalization. Augmented training with elastic deformations and intensity jitter is standard practice. Our method targets fine boundaries through contour-aware supervision and hybrid losses. We integrate lightweight attention to focus features near ambiguous interfaces. Experiments span BraTS, ACDC, and LiTS with rigorous cross-validation. Results demonstrate improved Dice and Hausdorff metrics while preserving efficiency.

Yet the evidence base that links graph learning to user-understandable explanations remains fragmented. We review representative approaches to collaborative filtering and knowledge transfer to situate our contribution. Zhang et al. These strands motivate our exploration of path-based rationales that trace how messages propagate from entities to delivered items. Our method unifies attribution with calibration, yielding verifiable explanations while preserving competitive accuracy.

Our framework operationalizes do-calculus proxies without access to structural graphs during training. Experiments demonstrate improved out-of-distribution robustness on VQA-CP and compositional splits with minimal overhead.

document: Automatic speech recognition in low-resource domains remains vulnerable to domain shift between training and deployment. Background noise, speaker demographics, and recording hardware induce distributional drift that impairs acoustic and language models. Recent advances in self-supervised pretraining and unsupervised adaptation offer partial remedies but lack consistent evaluation. We focus on test-time strategies that adapt normalization and confidence without updating decoder parameters. To contextualize our design, we outline representative adaptations from the transfer learning literature. Sun and Saenko (2016) match second-order statistics using CORAL. Tzeng et al. (2017) decouple representation learning and adaptation with discriminative objectives. Baevski et al. (2020) learn speech representations with wav2vec 2.0 pretraining on unlabeled audio at massive scale. Together these lines leave open questions about aligning latent speech features without labeled target transcriptions.
